title: Pharmacology Part B: Systems & Safety
description: Endocrine (Insulin), Respiratory, Immunologic, Chemo, Herbal Safety

questions.text: Metformin primarily works by:
options: Stimulating the pancreas to squeeze more insulin
Increasing insulin sensitivity and decreasing liver glucose production
questions.explanation: Metformin is a Biguanide. It sensitizes cells to insulin and tells the liver to stop dumping sugar into the blood. No hypoglycemia risk alone.

questions.text: Rapid-Acting Insulin (Homalog/Lispro) has an onset of:
questions.explanation: Fastest insulin. Food MUST be in front of the patient. Onset 5-15 min.

questions.text: Long-Acting Insulin (Glargine/Lantus) is unique because:
options: It has NO peak (steady plateau for ~24 hours)
questions.explanation: Glargine has no peak. It provides a steady basal rate of insulin coverage for 24 hours. Never mix it in a syringe.

questions.text: Corticosteroids (Prednisone) reduce inflammation but cause:
questions.explanation: Corticosteroids powerfully suppress the immune system. Long term use leads to Cushing's, infection risk, hyperglycemia, and bone loss.

questions.text: What is 'Neutropenia' in chemotherapy patients?
options: Low Neutrophils (WBCs), leading to life-threatening infection risk
questions.explanation: Chemo kills fast-growing cells, including bone marrow. Neutropenia means the body has no defense against bacteria -> Sepsis risk.

questions.text: Cyclosporine is an immunosuppressant used to:
questions.explanation: Cyclosporine targets T-lymphocytes to stop the body from attacking a new organ.

questions.text: Chemotherapy (Alkylating agents) target:
options: ALL rapidly dividing cells (Cancer, Hair, GI tract, Bone Marrow)
questions.explanation: Chemo attacks rapid division. This explains the side effects: Hair loss (follicles), Nausea (GI lining), Infection (Bone marrow).

questions.text: Regular Insulin (Short Acting) is the only insulin that can be given:
questions.explanation: Regular insulin is the only one safe for IV administration (e.g., for DKA).

questions.text: Beta Adrenergic Agonists (Albuterol) cause bronchodilation but side effects include:
questions.explanation: Because they mimic adrenaline (sympathetic NS stimulation), they speed up the heart and cause jitters.

questions.text: Why must patients on long-term steroids NOT stop abruptly?
options: Adrenal Crisis (The body stopped making its own cortisol)
questions.explanation: Exogenous steroids supress the HPA axis. Stopping suddenly leads to Adrenal Crisis because the body can't produce cortisol fast enough.

questions.text: Theophylline (Methylxanthine) is a bronchodilator with a risk of:
questions.explanation: Like Digoxin, Theophylline has a narrow window. Toxicity prevents as nervousness, seizures, and arrhythmias.

questions.text: NPH Insulin is intermediate-acting and appears:
questions.explanation: NPH is Cloudy. (Remember: 'Clear before Cloudy' when mixing).

questions.text: What is the primary role of ACE Inhibitors in Heart Failure?
questions.explanation: By blocking Angiotensin II (a potent vasoconstrictor), ACEs open arteries, making it easier for the weak heart to pump.

questions.text: Which class of drugs is used to 'dry up' secretions (Anticholinergic)?
questions.explanation: Anticholinergics block acetylcholine, leading to 'Can't See, Can't Pee, Can't Spit, Can't Sh*t' effects.

questions.text: What is the 'synergy' of using a Loop Diuretic + Potassium-Sparing Diuretic?
options: Balancing Potassium loss (Loop) with Potassium retention (Sparing)
questions.explanation: They cancel out each other's potassium side effects while both removing fluid.

questions.text: A patient on Warfarin has an INR of 5.0 (High). The risk is:
questions.explanation: High INR = Thin blood = Bleeding risk. Low INR = Thick blood = Clotting risk.

questions.text: Calcium Channel Blockers avoid grapefruit juice because:
options: Grapefruit inhibits CYP450 enzymes causing toxic drug levels
questions.explanation: Grapefruit Juice inhibits the liver enzyme that breaks down CCBs (and Statins), leading to toxic overdose levels.

questions.text: What is 'Palliative Chemotherapy'?
options: Treatment focused on symptom relief and quality of life, not cure
questions.explanation: Palliative chemo aims to shrink tumors to reduce pain/obstruction, extending quality of life rather than eradicating the disease.

questions.text: Immunosuppressants make patients vulnerable to 'Opportunistic Infections', which are:
options: Infections caused by common organisms that usually don't harm healthy people
questions.explanation: Organisms like Candida or CMV that a healthy immune system ignores become deadly when suppressed.

questions.text: The onset of NPH insulin peak is typically:
questions.explanation: NPH peaks mid-day (6-12 hours). This is when the risk of hypoglycemia is highest if they haven't eaten lunch.

questions.text: Sublingual Nitroglycerin is given for Chest Pain. It works by:
options: Systemic Vasodilation (reducing Preload) and Coronary Artery Dilation
questions.explanation: Nitro causes massive vasodilation. It drops blood pressure (reducing heart work) and opens heart arteries.

questions.text: Which organ is primarily responsible for Drug Excretion?
questions.explanation: The Kidneys filter blood and excrete drug metabolites. Renal failure = Drug Toxicity.

questions.text: Which organ is primarily responsible for Drug Metabolism?
questions.explanation: The Liver is the chemical processing plant (Cytochrome P450 system).

questions.text: What does 'Teratogenic' mean?
questions.explanation: Teratogens cause malformations in a developing fetus (e.g., ACE inhibitors, Retinoids, Thalidomide).

questions.text: Why are 'Statins' given at night?
options: Cholesterol synthesis by the liver is highest at night
questions.explanation: The liver makes the most cholesterol while we sleep, so HMG-CoA reductase inhibitors work best then.
